summary: Gruber highlights a concerning Orrin Hatch amendment buried in the Homeland Security bill that would make it easier for ISPs to disclose email communications without a warrant. He frames this as a post-election consequence, with Republicans now empowered to further erode digital privacy protections that were already weakened by the Patriot Act. The sardonic reference to Phil Zimmerman — creator of PGP encryption — underscores the widening gap between government surveillance ambitions and privacy advocacy.
key_points: An Orrin Hatch amendment hidden in the Homeland Security bill would let ISPs disclose emails without warrants
This further erodes privacy protections already weakened by the Patriot Act
The Republican election victory enables passage of legislation hostile to digital privacy
The Phil Zimmerman reference highlights the tension between encryption advocacy and government surveillance expansion
money_quotes: I take it the Republicans won't be conferring any awards on Phil Zimmerman any time soon.
key_insight: Election outcomes have immediate, concrete consequences for digital privacy, as victorious politicians waste no time burying surveillance expansions in must-pass legislation.
